body.title: Measure-first: does ticket detail correlate with build cost? Then eval Opus-written tickets
body.result: Phase 1 analysis: r=+0.362 (wrong direction, size confound); STOP — no Opus ticket pipeline until N>50 + reset data present; 18 tests
body.intention: I intend that we know whether richer tickets actually build cheaper before investing in an Opus ticket-writing pipeline.
body.description:
Hypothesis: more detailed tickets are easier/cheaper to build. Today's evidence says the bottleneck is verification, not detail — so test the hypothesis before building anything. Phase 1 (cheap, do first): mine existing data — for each ticket in sprint_tokens.log (build cost = turns + output tokens) pull its description + reset_count from the queue, compute a detail proxy (description length, concrete vs TBD affected-files, presence of real completion criteria), and check whether detail correlates with lower build cost / fewer resets. If there's NO correlation, stop — the Opus-ticket pipeline is premature optimization. Phase 2 (only if Phase 1 confirms): run an /eval-run comparing Opus-written tickets vs the old tickets on a shared metric: reset_count + sprint tokens + completion-audit pass-rate (from T-completion-audit-closed-tickets).

**Affected files:** a new analysis script under lab/claudecode/ (reads sprint_tokens.log + queue); /eval-run integration for Phase 2
**Design rules:** none apply
**Scope boundary:** Phase 1 (data mine + correlation) is the deliverable of THIS ticket. Phase 2 (Opus-ticket pipeline + eval) is gated on Phase 1 showing correlation and gets its own ticket. Out of scope: building the Opus-ticket pipeline before the data justifies it.
**Completion criteria:** Phase 1 script outputs, per closed ticket, (detail proxy, build cost, reset_count) and a correlation summary; a clear yes/no on whether ticket detail predicts build cost; recommendation on whether to proceed to Phase 2.
